inputs.story: Nicholas and Edith are having a small dinner party this evening at their home in Cedar Valley, and they've decided to open a select number of rare wines from their personal collection to celebrate the occasion. Using only the clues below, match each wine to its vintage (year) and vineyard, and determine the type of each. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. The Luzagueil is a chardonnay.
2. The Annata Branco is either the 1992 wine or the syrah.
3. The Friambliss is a syrah.
4. Of the pinot gris and the 1984 bottle, one is the Luzagueil and the other is the Zifennwein.
5. The pinot gris was bottled 4 years after the merlot.
inputs.constants:
vintages: 1984; 1988; 1992; 1996.
wines: Annata Branco; Friambliss; Luzagueil; Zifennwein.
types: chardonnay; merlot; pinot gris; syrah.
inputs.solution:
Vintages	Wines	Types
1984	Luzagueil	chardonnay
1988	Friambliss	syrah
1992	Annata Branco	merlot
1996	Zifennwein	pinot gris

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
Nicholas and Edith are having a small dinner party this evening at their home in Cedar Valley, and they've decided to open a select number of rare wines from their personal collection to celebrate the occasion. Using only the clues below, match each wine to its vintage (year) and vineyard, and determine the type of each. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The Luzagueil is a chardonnay.
2. The Annata Branco is either the 1992 wine or the syrah.
3. The Friambliss is a syrah.
4. Of the pinot gris and the 1984 bottle, one is the Luzagueil and the other is the Zifennwein.
5. The pinot gris was bottled 4 years after the merlot.

Constants:
vintages: 1984; 1988; 1992; 1996.
wines: Annata Branco; Friambliss; Luzagueil; Zifennwein.
types: chardonnay; merlot; pinot gris; syrah.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

asp_generation.thinking:
Here's a thinking process:

1.  **Analyze the Problem:**
   - **Goal:** Match each wine to its vintage (year) and type.
   - **Entities/Categories:**
     - Wines: Annata Branco, Friambliss, Luzagueil, Zifennwein
     - Vintages: 1984, 1988, 1992, 1996
     - Types: chardonnay, merlot, pinot gris, syrah
   - **Clues:**
     1. The Luzagueil is a chardonnay.
     2. The Annata Branco is either the 1992 wine or the syrah.
     3. The Friambliss is a syrah.
     4. Of the pinot gris and the 1984 bottle, one is the Luzagueil and the other is the Zifennwein.
     5. The pinot gris was bottled 4 years after the merlot.

2.  **Map to ASP Concepts:**
   - Categories: `wine`, `vintage`, `type`.
   - Constants:
     - `wine("Annata Branco"; "Friambliss"; "Luzagueil"; "Zifennwein").`
     - `vintage(1984; 1988; 1992; 1996).`
     - `type("chardonnay"; "merlot"; "pinot gris"; "syrah").`
   - Predicate: `match(W, V, T)` or `wine(W, V, T)`. Let's use `match(W, V, T)`.
   - Search space: `{match(W, V, T): vintage(V), type(T)} = 1 :- wine(W).`
   - Uniqueness: `{W1=W2; V1=V2; T1=T2} = 0 :- match(W1,V1,T1), match(W2,V2,T2), (W1,V1,T1)!=(W2,V2,T2).`

3.  **Encode Clues:**
   - *Clue 1:* The Luzagueil is a chardonnay.
     - `T = "chardonnay" :- match(W, V, T), W = "Luzagueil".`
   - *Clue 2:* The Annata Branco is either the 1992 wine or the syrah.
     - `{V = 1992; T = "syrah"} = 1 :- match(W, V, T), W = "Annata Branco".`
   - *Clue 3:* The Friambliss is a syrah.
     - `T = "syrah" :- match(W, V, T), W = "Friambliss".`
   - *Clue 4:* Of the pinot gris and the 1984 bottle, one is the Luzagueil and the other is the Zifennwein.
     - This means:
       - The pinot gris is either Luzagueil or Zifennwein.
       - The 1984 bottle is either Luzagueil or Zifennwein.
       - They are different wines (Luzagueil != Zifennwein).
     - Let's break it down carefully:
       - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" usually implies:
         - A != B (pinot gris wine != 1984 wine)
         - A is C or D
         - B is C or D
       - So:
         - `W1 != W2 :- match(W1, V1, T1), match(W2, V2, T2), T1 = "pinot gris", V2 = 1984.`
         - `{W1 = "Luzagueil"; W1 = "Zifennwein"} = 1 :- match(W1, V1, T1), T1 = "pinot gris".`
         - `{W2 = "Luzagueil"; W2 = "Zifennwein"} = 1 :- match(W2, V2, T2), V2 = 1984.`
     - Wait, the phrasing "Of the pinot gris and the 1984 bottle, one is the Luzagueil and the other is the Zifennwein." implies exactly that mapping. It's a standard logic puzzle pattern.
     - Let's verify with the rules: "Common patterns: 'Of A and B, one is C and the other is D' → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D."
     - So I will follow exactly that.
   - *Clue 5:* The pinot gris was bottled 4 years after the merlot.
     - This means: `V_pinot_gris = V_merlot + 4`.
     - Also implies they are different wines, but that's covered by uniqueness.
     - `V1 = V2 + 4 :- match(W1, V1, T1), match(W2, V2, T2), T1 = "pinot gris", T2 = "merlot".`

4.  **Check Constraints & Edge Cases:**
   - Vintages: 1984, 1988, 1992, 1996. Differences of 4 years: (1984,1988), (1988,1992), (1992,1996).
   - Clue 5 says pinot gris is 4 years after merlot. So possible pairs: (merlot:1984, pg:1988), (merlot:1988, pg:1992), (merlot:1992, pg:1996).
   - Clue 1: Luzagueil is chardonnay.
   - Clue 3: Friambliss is syrah.
   - Clue 2: Annata Branco is 1992 or syrah.
   - Clue 4: pinot gris and 1984 are Luzagueil and Zifennwein.
     - Since Luzagueil is chardonnay (Clue 1), pinot gris cannot be Luzagueil. Therefore, pinot gris must be Zifennwein, and 1984 must be Luzagueil.
     - Let's see if the ASP encoding handles this logically without hardcoding. Yes, the rules will force it.
     - Wait, if pinot gris is Zifennwein, then Zifennwein is pinot gris.
     - If 1984 is Luzagueil, then Luzagueil is 1984.
     - Luzagueil is chardonnay (Clue 1). So Luzagueil = 1984 = chardonnay.
     - Friambliss = syrah.
     - Annata Branco is 1992 or syrah. Since Friambliss is syrah, Annata Branco cannot be syrah (unique types). So Annata Branco must be 1992.
     - Then Zifennwein (pinot gris) must be the remaining vintage. Vintages used: 1984 (Luzagueil), 1992 (Annata Branco). Remaining: 1988, 1996.
     - Pinot gris (Zifennwein) is 4 years after merlot. Merlot must be 1988, Pinot gris 1992? No, 1992 is Annata Branco. So Merlot=1988, Pinot Gris=1992? Wait, Annata Branco is 1992. Can Annata Branco be pinot gris? Yes, types and wines are separate categories. But we already have Zifennwein = pinot gris from Clue 4 deduction. Let's re-verify Clue 4 deduction.
     - Clue 4: "Of the pinot gris and the 1984 bottle, one is the Luzagueil and the other is the Zifennwein."
     - This means: (pinot gris == Luzagueil OR pinot gris == Zifennwein) AND (1984 == Luzagueil OR 1984 == Zifennwein). And they are different.
     - Since Luzagueil is chardonnay, pinot gris != Luzagueil. So pinot gris == Zifennwein.
     - Thus, 1984 == Luzagueil.
     - So we have:
       - Luzagueil: 1984, chardonnay
       - Zifennwein: pinot gris
       - Friambliss: syrah
       - Annata Branco: 1992 (from clue 2, since syrah is taken)
     - Remaining wine: Zifennwein. Remaining type for Annata Branco? Not syrah, not chardonnay, not pinot gris (taken by Zifennwein). So Annata Branco must be merlot.
     - If Annata Branco is merlot, and it's 1992, then pinot gris (Zifennwein) is 4 years after = 1996.
     - Friambliss gets remaining vintage: 1988.
     - Let's check consistency:
       - Luzagueil: 1984, chardonnay
       - Friambliss: 1988, syrah
       - Annata Branco: 1992, merlot
       - Zifennwein: 1996, pinot gris
     - Clue 5: pinot gris (1996) bottled 4 years after merlot (1992). Matches.
     - All clues satisfied. Unique solution exists.

5.  **Draft ASP Code:**
   ```asp
   % Constants
   wine("Annata Branco"; "Friambliss"; "Luzagueil"; "Zifennwein").
   vintage(1984; 1988; 1992; 1996).
   type("chardonnay"; "merlot"; "pinot gris"; "syrah").

   % Search space
   {match(W, V, T): vintage(V), type(T)} = 1 :- wine(W).

   % Uniqueness
   {W1=W2; V1=V2; T1=T2} = 0 :- match(W1, V1, T1), match(W2, V2, T2), (W1, V1, T1) != (W2, V2, T2).

   % 1. The Luzagueil is a chardonnay.
   T = "chardonnay" :- match(W, V, T), W = "Luzagueil".

   % 2. The Annata Branco is either the 1992 wine or the syrah.
   {V = 1992; T = "syrah"} = 1 :- match(W, V, T), W = "Annata Branco".

   % 3. The Friambliss is a syrah.
   T = "syrah" :- match(W, V, T), W = "Friambliss".

   % 4. Of the pinot gris and the 1984 bottle, one is the Luzagueil and the other is the Zifennwein.
   W1 != W2 :- match(W1, V1, T1), match(W2, V2, T2), T1 = "pinot gris", V2 = 1984.
   {W1 = "Luzagueil"; W1 = "Zifennwein"} = 1 :- match(W1, V1, T1), T1 = "pinot gris".
   {W2 = "Luzagueil"; W2 = "Zifennwein"} = 1 :- match(W2, V2, T2), V2 = 1984.

   % 5. The pinot gris was bottled 4 years after the merlot.
   V1 = V2 + 4 :- match(W1, V1, T1), match(W2, V2, T2), T1 = "pinot gris", T2 = "merlot".

   #show match/3.
   ```

6.  **Review against Rules:**
   - Rule 1: Constants declared correctly. `vintage` uses integers. `wine` and `type` use quoted strings.
   - Rule 2: Predicate `match/3` used. All categories appear.
   - Rule 3: Search space `{match(W, V, T): vintage(V), type(T)} = 1 :- wine(W).` matches pattern.
   - Rule 4: Uniqueness `{W1=W2; V1=V2; T1=T2} = 0 :- match(W1, V1, T1), match(W2, V2, T2), (W1, V1, T1) != (W2, V2, T2).` matches pattern.
   - Rule 5: Clues encoded correctly.
     - Clue 4 follows the exact pattern specified: `W1 != W2 :- ...`, `{W1 = ...; W1 = ...} = 1 :- ...`, `{W2 = ...; W2 = ...} = 1 :- ...`
     - Clue 5 uses arithmetic `V1 = V2 + 4`. This is valid in Clingo.
   - Rule 6: `#show match/3.` present.

I'll stick to `!=` as in examples. Actually, example 1 uses `W1=W2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0` and `(E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2)`. Example 2 uses `C1!=C2`. So `!=` is fine.

```asp
% Constants
wine("Annata Branco"; "Friambliss"; "Luzagueil"; "Zifennwein").
vintage(1984; 1988; 1992; 1996).
type("chardonnay"; "merlot"; "pinot gris"; "syrah").

% Search space
{match(W, V, T): vintage(V), type(T)} = 1 :- wine(W).

% Uniqueness
{W1=W2; V1=V2; T1=T2} = 0 :- match(W1, V1, T1), match(W2, V2, T2), (W1, V1, T1) != (W2, V2, T2).

% 1. The Luzagueil is a chardonnay.
T = "chardonnay" :- match(W, V, T), W = "Luzagueil".

% 2. The Annata Branco is either the 1992 wine or the syrah.
{V = 1992; T = "syrah"} = 1 :- match(W, V, T), W = "Annata Branco".

% 3. The Friambliss is a syrah.
T = "syrah" :- match(W, V, T), W = "Friambliss".

% 4. Of the pinot gris and the 1984 bottle, one is the Luzagueil and the other is the Zifennwein.
W1 != W2 :- match(W1, V1, T1), match(W2, V2, T2), T1 = "pinot gris", V2 = 1984.
{W1 = "Luzagueil"; W1 = "Zifennwein"} = 1 :- match(W1, V1, T1), T1 = "pinot gris".
{W2 = "Luzagueil"; W2 = "Zifennwein"} = 1 :- match(W2, V2, T2), V2 = 1984.

% 5. The pinot gris was bottled 4 years after the merlot.
V1 = V2 + 4 :- match(W1, V1, T1), match(W2, V2, T2), T1 = "pinot gris", T2 = "merlot".

#show match/3.
   ```
